Q1: I organized my study routine around what I could physically do with the material. Being a kinesthetic learner, I'd often print things out or work through problems by hand rather than just reading on screen. I tried to dedicate about 17 hours a week, usually broken into chunks between my other responsibilities. I'd set up at the kitchen table with my notes spread out, sometimes with a coffee, and just work through things practically. It wasn't always perfectly scheduled, but I had a general rhythm that worked for me.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due and what else was going on in my life. Some weeks I'd be really consistent, studying a bit each day, but other weeks everything would pile up and I'd have to cram more into the weekend. With limited resources, I also had to work around when I could access the internet or get to campus. Deadlines were the main driver of when I'd intensify my efforts, honestly.
Q3: Usually I logged into the LMS when I needed to check assignment details or see if there were any announcements. Sometimes I'd go in specifically to watch lecture videos or download materials I could study offline later. If I saw a discussion post notification, that might prompt me to log in too. It wasn't always a daily habit, more like when I had a specific purpose in mind.
Q4: There was a period mid-semester where logging in became more automatic for me. I'd wake up, have breakfast, and check the LMS like I was checking messages. That happened because I realized I'd missed an important update once, and I didn't want that to happen again. The routine stuck for a while, especially when there were regular weekly activities posted. But it faded a bit toward the end when things got busier.
Q5: The video lectures were genuinely helpful because I could pause and rewatch sections while I practiced problems. I watched quite a lot of video content because it helped me understand concepts I could then apply hands-on. The discussion forums were also useful when classmates explained things in different ways. I appreciated anything that let me engage actively rather than just passively reading.
Q6: I often skipped the supplementary readings or extra articles that weren't directly tied to assignments. With my limited resources, I had to be strategic about what I spent time on, and those felt like extras I couldn't afford to prioritize. Some of the longer documents I'd download intending to read later but never got around to. If something wasn't clearly connected to my grade, it usually got pushed aside.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to understanding the material better or improving my grade. Anything that let me practice and apply concepts felt valuable to me as a hands-on learner. If I could tell it would help me on exams or assignments, I was motivated to engage. Vague or overly theoretical stuff without clear application felt less worthwhile.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different because there was less pressure attached to them. I'd sometimes look at them and think they'd be nice to do, but required work always came first. Without the accountability of a deadline or grade, optional stuff often got pushed to the bottom of my list. It's not that I didn't value them, but with everything else going on, I had to prioritize.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my real studying happened offline. I'd download lecture slides and PDFs, then work through them on paper away from my computer. I made physical flashcards and practiced problems by hand because that's how I learn best. I also used some textbooks from the library when I could get them. The LMS was more of a starting point than where all my learning happened.
Q10: There were many times I'd be studying from printed notes or working through practice problems at home, and none of that would show up anywhere online. I'd spend hours reviewing material I'd already downloaded, making diagrams, or teaching concepts to myself out loud. Sometimes I'd study with a classmate in person, talking through problems together. All of that learning was real but completely invisible to the system.
Q11: Yes, this was pretty common for me. I'd access a video once to download it or take notes, then review those notes multiple times afterward. Same with lecture slides—I'd open them once on the LMS, save them, and then study from the saved version repeatedly. My actual engagement with the material was much deeper than one click would suggest.
Q12: There were definitely harder moments, usually when personal stuff got overwhelming or when I felt confused about the material. At one point, I was dealing with some financial stress and it was hard to focus on coursework. When concepts built on each other and I felt lost, it became tempting to disengage. Those periods were tough, but I tried to push through.
Q13: Yes, all of those things affected how I used the LMS. When I was tired or stressed, I'd log in but not really absorb much. Competing responsibilities like work and family stuff meant sometimes the LMS was the last thing on my mind. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those sections temporarily. I had to balance a lot, and the LMS engagement reflected that reality.
Q14: There were times I'd open materials and scroll through them without really processing anything. I might watch a video but realize I hadn't absorbed it because I was too tired or distracted. It's like being physically present but mentally elsewhere. Those sessions felt unproductive even though they'd show up as activity in the system.
Q15: I definitely postponed things, especially assignments that felt overwhelming or unclear. Sometimes I knew something was important but couldn't muster the energy or focus to tackle it right away. With my low motivation at times, starting felt like the hardest part. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and sometimes that cycle repeated for a while before I finally got going.
Q16: Deadlines were huge motivators for me—they basically dictated when I'd ramp up my LMS usage. In the days before something was due, I'd be logging in more frequently, checking requirements, and making sure I had everything I needed. Without deadlines, I'd probably drift and not engage as consistently. They provided the structure I needed to stay on track.
Q17: Feedback did affect my engagement, especially when it was encouraging or gave me clear direction. After getting decent marks on something, I'd feel more motivated to keep going. But if feedback was delayed or vague, it was harder to know if I was on the right track. The CBT sessions I had actually helped me process feedback more constructively and stay positive.
Q18: The course structure gave me a framework to follow, which was helpful. Weekly modules and clear deadlines made it easier to plan my time. When things were well-organized, I felt more confident about what I needed to do. But when the structure was unclear or materials were scattered, it was harder to stay engaged and I'd feel lost.
Q19: Having materials clearly labeled and organized made engagement much easier for me. The video content being accessible was great for my learning style. What made things harder was when there were too many platforms or when important information was buried in long documents. Simplicity and clarity in design really helped me stay on track.
Q20: I think students might be exploring, trying to figure out what's expected, or looking for resources without actually completing work yet. They could also be struggling with understanding and clicking around trying to find help. Sometimes high activity is procrastination—being busy without being productive. Or they might be dealing with personal issues that make it hard to follow through despite good intentions.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience somewhat. I did a lot of studying offline, so my LMS activity didn't capture everything I was doing. My exam score was decent even though my task completion percentage wasn't great. I think some students are just efficient—they know what they need and don't spend extra time clicking around. Quality of engagement matters more than quantity of clicks.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when students access materials and how often they log in. They can track video watch time and participation in discussions. But they miss the depth of understanding, offline studying, and the quality of engagement. They can't capture whether someone was truly focused or just had the page open. The emotional and cognitive aspects of learning are invisible to logs.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actively thinking about the material, connecting it to what I already know, and trying to apply it. It's not just showing up or clicking through—it's being mentally invested and curious. For me, engagement feels like when I'm working through a problem with my hands and suddenly something clicks. It's that feeling of genuine involvement in learning.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show the hours I spent with printed materials, the study sessions with classmates, or the times I practiced concepts physically. It wouldn't capture how the CBT sessions helped me manage stress and stay positive about my studies. It also wouldn't show the personal challenges I navigated while maintaining my grades. My real engagement was much richer than any log could reflect.
